source_metadata.title_raw: Gary S. Winuk, Gabriel Ravel - A-19-224 - December 10, 2019 - Sacramento

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 10, 2019

Gary S. Winuk
Kaufman Legal Group
A Professional Corporation
621 Capitol Mall, Suite 1900
Sacramento, CA 95814

Gabriel Ravel
CA Health and Human Services Agency
1600 9th St Ste 460
Sacramento, CA 95814-6439

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-19-224

Dear Mr. Winuk and Mr. Ravel:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Government Code Section 1090, et seq.¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the Act and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code.

Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

We are required to forward your request regarding Section 1090 and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Do the conflict of interest provisions under the Act or Section 1090 prohibit Dr. Christina Ghaly or her spouse, Dr. Mark Ghaly, from participating in contracts between the County of Los

_______________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-224
Page No. 2

Angeles and constituent Departments of the California Health and Human Services Agency
("CHHS") where Dr. Christina Ghaly is the Director of Health Services for the County and Dr.
Mark Ghaly is the Secretary of CHHS?

CONCLUSION

No. As explained below, neither the Act nor Section 1090 prohibits Dr. Christina Ghaly or
Dr. Mark Ghaly from participating in contracts between the County and constituent Departments of
CHHS.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You represent the County of Los Angeles, CHHS, and their employees, Drs. Christina and
Mark Ghaly. Dr. Christina Ghaly has served as the Director of the Los Angeles County Department
of Health Services ("DHS") since September 2018. Her spouse, Dr. Mark Ghaly, was appointed by
Governor Gavin Newsom in March 2019 to serve as the Secretary of CHHS, which oversees the
work of several State Departments that are separate governmental entities, including the State
Department of Health Care Services ("DHCS").

The County of Los Angeles sends funding to, and receives funding from, DHCS, and that
funding most often is provided via written agreements or contracts. The agreements between the
State and the County are made between DHCS and the County Board of Supervisors ("Board").
The Board holds the legal authority to enter into contracts on the County's behalf, and it is
permitted to delegate that authority to an authorized designee.

As a standard practice, DHS asks the Board for delegated authority to permit the DHS
Director to sign all such contracts between the Board and State departments with limited
exceptions. The DHS Director then delegates that signature authority to a DHS manager. However,
the DHS Director does sign some of the contracts personally and, if necessary, DHS can request
that a person other than the DHS Director be authorized to sign on the County's behalf.

DHS is involved intermittently with DHCS in discussing discrete issues pertaining to health
care policy setting and the design of the health care financing structure that exists under Federal
Medicaid laws and regulations when those design issues involve the County's Medicaid
reimbursement for health care services. These policies and financing structures can be
memorialized in State Plans, Amendments to State Plans, Federal Waivers – all pursuant to
Medicaid laws, and in state statutes. These contracts, which can be signed by the DHS Director,
or any other designee in the Board's discretion, are ministerial and provide for the mechanism by
which funds between the County and DHCS are transmitted, as set forth and pursuant to these State
Plans, Amendments, Waivers and state law.

The Whole Person Care Pilot (WPC) program exemplifies this process. Pursuant to Section
1115(a) of the Social Security Act, California received a state-wide Federal Medicaid Waiver to
implement this program, which focused on local and regional efforts to integrate health care
delivery systems and improve the care provided to Medi-Cal's most high-risk beneficiaries.
DHS's Chief Financial Officer and a DHS Deputy Director largely negotiated the terms of Los

File No. A-19-224
Page No. 3

Angeles County's participation in the WPC program and involved the DHS Director, Dr. Christina
Ghaly's predecessor, as necessary to ensure that the Waiver's terms reflected the Director's policy
objectives. DHCS and the County then entered into a contract specifying the terms and conditions
of the Waiver, which the Board approved. The Board delegated signature authority for the contract
to the DHS Director, who ultimately executed it.

Dr. Christina Ghaly delegates day-to-day management of agreements involving the State to
her subordinates, including review, consultation with counsel, and working with DHCS to make
any needed contract revisions to conform with the State Plan, Amendments, Waivers or state law.
She does not devise or negotiate the terms of such contracts, although she does periodically review
them before signing. The contracts with the State that are managed by DHS are formally received
by DHS only as a result of delegated authority from the Board, which is the contracting party.

Dr. Mark Ghaly is not directly involved in the formulation or negotiation of contracts
between the constituent departments of CHHS and the Board or its constituent departments.
However, he does periodically participate in making state policy that may ultimately be
implemented through such contracts. For example, he may be involved in developing new
legislation or budget change proposals to improve existing grant programs for counties that are
administered by CHHS departments. CHHS does not directly contract with the County.

Neither Dr. Christina Ghaly nor Dr. Mark Ghaly receives any financial benefit from the
contracts between CHHS's constituent State Departments and the County.

ANALYSIS

The Act

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section.

Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. Most pertinent
to the analysis are the following:

• An interest in any source of income to the official aggregating $500 or more in
value provided to, received by, or promised to, the public official within 12 months prior to
the decision.

• An interest in a business entity in which the public official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.

Dr. Christina Ghaly is the Director of the DHS and Dr. Mark Ghaly serves as the Secretary
of the CHHS. While both receive income as government officials, under Section 82030(b)(2)
salary, reimbursement for expenses, and per diem from a governmental agency are not considered
income for purposes of the Act. Additionally, government agencies, such as the DHS and the

File No. A-19-224
Page No. 4

CHHS, are not organizations or enterprises operated for profit. Therefore, neither Dr. Christina
Ghaly nor Dr. Mark Ghaly has an interest in their respective agencies as a source of income or as a
business entity. Barring any other interest in a particular decision, they are not prohibited from
taking part in the decisions related to contracts between the County and a constituent Department of
CHHS.2

Section 1090

Section 1090 provides that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial
district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by
them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members."

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) The financial interest
cannot be interpreted in a technical manner, and a prohibited financial interest is one that, whether
small or indirect, has the potential to divide an official's loyalties and compromise the undivided
representation of the public interests. (Lexin v. Superior Court, 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1075.) An official
has an interest in the community and separate property income of his or her spouse. (Nielsen v.
Richards (1925) 75 Cal.App. 680; Thorpe v. Long Beach Community College Dist. (2000) 83
Cal.App.4th 655; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 69 (2006).)

With respect to making a contract, Section 1090 reaches beyond the officials who
participate personally in the actual execution of the contract to capture those officials who
participate in any way in the making of the contract:

The decisional law, therefore, has not interpreted section 1090 in a
hypertechnical manner, but holds that an official (or a public employee)
may be convicted of violation no matter whether he actually participated
personally in the execution of the questioned contract, if it is established
that he had the opportunity to, and did, influence execution directly or
indirectly to promote his personal interests.

(People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052.)

Therefore, participation in the making of a contract is defined broadly as any act involving
preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and
specifications, and solicitation for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae
(1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237.)

Here, with limited exceptions, Dr. Christina Ghaly as the Director of DHS has the delegated
authority to sign all contracts between the County and State departments. As the Secretary of
CHHS, Dr. Mark Ghaly can participate in making state policy that is ultimately implemented
through such contracts. Because both of the Ghaly's have the opportunity in their respective official

2 While we note that an official also has a financial interest in his or her personal finances (Section 87103;
Regulation 18702.5), there is also no indication that the types of potential decisions at issue would foreseeably affect
the Ghalys' personal finances.

File No. A-19-224
Page No. 5

capacities to influence these contracts, they participate in the making of such contracts for purposes
of Section 1090.

Next, in order to determine whether Dr. Christina Ghaly or Dr. Mark Ghaly has a financial
interest in any contract between the County and a constituent Department of CHHS, such as DHCS,
it is appropriate to look to the definitions of the noninterest exceptions contained in Section 1091.5
for guidance in determining what falls within the scope of the term "financial interest" as used in
Section 1090. (See 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2002).)³

The Legislature has created certain exceptions which relate to government income and
officeholding. Pertinent to the facts presented, Section 1091.5(a)(9) provides that an officer or
employee has a noninterest in a contract if his or her interest in that contract is:

That of a person receiving salary, per diem, or reimbursement for
expenses from a government entity, unless the contract directly involves
the department of the government entity that employs the officer or
employee, provided that the interest is disclosed to the body or board at
the time of consideration of the contract, and provided further that the
interest is noted in its official record.

In Lexin, supra, 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1081, the California Supreme Court determined that this
exception applies when "the contract involves no direct financial gain, does not directly affect the
official's employing department, and is only with the general government entity for which the
official works." The Court went on to state that "Section 1091.5(a)(9) excuses conflicts that arise
from the identity of the party with or on whose behalf one is contracting (one's employer, other
than one's own specific department), but not conflicts that arise from the actual terms of the
contract." (Ibid.)

The Lexin Court then summarized the "logical statutory scheme" with respect to
government income and officeholding:

If a contract an official considers in his or her official capacity
is with the official's government employer and involves direct
financial gain, the official is prohibited from participating under
section 1090. If the contract involves no direct financial gain, but is
with or affects the official's own department, the official's interest is
a remote interest under section 1091, subdivision (b)(13) and subject
to the disclosure and recusal requirements of section 1091. Finally, if
the contract involves no direct financial gain, does not directly affect
the official's employing department, and is only with the general
government entity for which the official works, the interest is a
minimal or noninterest under section 1091.5(a)(9) and no conflict of
interest prohibition applies.

(Lexin, supra, 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1081.)

³ The remote interests in Section 1091 apply only to the members of a body or board, and do not apply to
employees. Therefore, none of the remote interests would apply to either Dr. Christina Ghaly or Dr. Mark Ghaly.

File No. A-19-224
Page No. 6

Thus, an officer or employee has a noninterest in his or her government-entity employer
under Section 1091.5(a)(9) if: (1) the contract at issue involves no direct financial gain to the
official or employee; (2) the contract does not directly affect the official's or employee's
department; (3) the interest is disclosed to the body or board at the time the contract is considered;
and (4) the interest is noted in the body or board's official record.

According to your facts, Dr. Christina Ghaly, the Director of DHS, would have the
delegated authority to sign contracts between the County and a constituent Department of CHHS
(e.g., DHCS), and neither of the Ghalys would receive any financial benefit from the contracts. You
cited to the WPC agreement between DHCS and the County concerning health care delivery
systems for Medi-Cal beneficiaries to exemplify the process for such contracts. There, the CFO and
Deputy Director of DHS largely negotiated the terms of the agreement on behalf of the County and
the former Director of DHS participated as necessary to certain policy objectives were met. The
County Board initially approved the terms and conditions of the program's Waiver and delegated
signature authority for the contract to the DHS Director.

Under the circumstances described above, Section 1091.5(a)(9) would apply to allow Dr.
Christina Ghaly to approve contracts with constituent Departments of CHHS, such as DHCS,
because such contracts would involve no direct financial gain to her or Dr. Mark Ghaly and
would not directly affect CHHS, Dr. Mark Ghaly's employing Department.

For the same reasons, Section 1091.5(a)(9) would apply to allow Dr. Mark Ghaly's
participation in providing policy guidance that informs the substantive provisions of the contracts
between CHHS's constituent Departments and the County.⁴ Such contracts would involve no direct
financial gain to either of the Ghalys and would not directly affect DHS, Dr. Christina Ghaly's
employing Department.

Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than non-interests or remote
interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in
furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.)
Where, as here, the Ghalys have a non-interest in contracts between CHHS's constituent
Departments and the County, the concerns underlying Section 1090 are simply not present.

Accordingly, where the contract at issue involves no direct financial gain to either Dr.
Christina Ghaly or Dr. Mark Ghaly, Section 1091.5(a)(9) applies to allow them to participate in
contracts between CHHS's constituent Departments and the County.

___________________________

⁴ The fact that neither of the Ghaly's is a member of a board or body does not change the result. The court in
Lexin contemplated this scenario when it explained that the exception is understood to apply only to contracts under
consideration between two public agencies—or, for example, where "the person is an employee of public agency A and a
board member of public agency B (or is otherwise in a position to influence agency B's decision) ...." (Lexin, supra, 47
Cal.4th 1050, 1082, emphasis added.) In addition, unlike remote interest exceptions under Section 1091, the noninterest
exceptions under Section 1091.5 are not expressly limited to members of a body or board.

File No. A-19-224
Page No. 7

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW:aja

capacities to influence these contracts, they participate in the making of such contracts for purposes 
of Section 1090.

government entity for which the official works, the interest is a 
minimal or noninterest under section 1091.5(a)(9) and no conflict of 
interest prohibition applies. 
(Lexin, supra, 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1081.) 
3 The remote interests in Section 1091 apply only to the members ofa body or board, and do not apply to 
employees. Therefore, none of the remote interests would apply to either Dr. Christina Ghaly or Dr. Mark Ghaly.  
Thus, an officer or employee has a noninterest in his or her government-entity employer 
under Section 1091.5(a)(9) if: (1) the contract at issue involves no direct financial gain to the 
official or employee; (2) the contract does not directly affect the official's or employee's 
department; (3) the interest is disclosed to the body or board at the time the contract is considered; 
and (4) the interest is noted in the body or board's official record.

delegated authority to sign contracts between the County and a constituent Department of CHHS

systems for Medi-Cal beneficiaries to exemplify the process for such contracts. There, the CFO and

County Board initially approved the terms and conditions of the program's Waiver and delegated 
signature authority for the contract to the DHS Director.

Christina Ghaly to approve contracts with constituent Departments of CHHS, such as DHCS, 
because such contracts would involve no direct financial gain to her or to Dr. Mark Ghaly and 
would not directly affect CHHS, Dr. Mark Ghaly's employing Department. 
For the same reasons, Section 1091.5(a)(9) would apply to allow Dr. Mark Ghaly's 
participation in providing policy guidance that informs the substantive provisions of the contracts 
between CHHS's constituent Departments and the County.4 Such contracts would involve no direct

Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than non-interests or remote 
interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in

consideration between two public agencies - for example, where "the person is an employee of public agency A and a

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the conflict of interest provisions under the Act or Section 1090 prohibit Dr. Christina 
Ghaly or her spouse, Dr. Mark Ghaly, from participating in contracts between the County of LosAngeles and constituent Departments of the California Health and Human Services Agency 
("CHHS") where Dr. Christina Ghaly is the Director of Health -Services for the County and Dr. 
Mark Ghaly is the Secretary of CHHS?

CONCLUSION: No. As explained below, neither the Act nor Section 1090 prohibits Dr. Christina Ghaly or 
Dr. Mark Ghaly from participating in contracts between the County and constituent Departments of 
CHHS.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 December 10, 2019 Gary S. Winuk Kaufman Legal Group A Professional Corporation 621 Capitol Mall, Suite 1900 Sacramento, CA 95814 Gabriel Ravel CA Health and Human Services Agency 1600 9th St Ste 460 Sacramento, CA 95814-6439 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-224 Dear Mr. Winuk and Mr. Ravel: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Government Code Section 1090, et seq.¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the Act and Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. We are required to forward your request regarding Section 1090 and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Los Angeles County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).) QUESTION Do the conflict of interest provisions under the Act or Section 1090 prohibit Dr. Christina Ghaly or her spouse, Dr. Mark Ghaly, from participating in contracts between the County of Los _______________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-19-224 Page No. 2 Angeles and constituent Departments of the California Health and Human Services Agency ("CHHS") where Dr. Christina Ghaly is the Director of Health Services for the County and Dr. Mark Ghaly is the Secretary of CHHS? CONCLUSION No. As explained below, neither the Act nor Section 1090 prohibits Dr. Christina Ghaly or Dr. Mark Ghaly from participating in contracts between the County and constituent Departments of CHHS. FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER You represent the County of Los Angeles, CHHS, and their employees, Drs. Christina and Mark Ghaly. Dr. Christina Ghaly has served as the Director of the Los Angeles County Department of Health Services ("DHS") since September 2018.
